title: Quick check: MX priority and targets

questions.prompt: A new client onboarding sets up two mail providers as MX records: their primary at priority 30 and their backup at priority 10. Which one will sending servers actually try first?

choices.label: The primary at 30; higher number means higher preference.
choices.rationale: MX priority is inverted: lower number = higher preference. This is the most common MX misconception.

choices: The backup at 10, because lower number = preferred.

choices.label: Both at once; MX delivers in parallel to all listed hosts.
choices.rationale: MX delivery is sequential by priority, not parallel. Senders try the lowest number first.

choices.label: Whichever the receiver's DNS cache returns first.
choices.rationale: Cache order doesn't determine MX preference. The priority field does.

questions.explanation: The values need swapping. Lower priority = higher preference (tried first). The 'primary at 30 / backup at 10' setup inverts the intent; senders try the backup first and only fall through to the primary if it's unreachable. Swap the numbers.

questions.prompt: A vendor's setup instructions ask you to publish an MX record pointing at `198.51.100.42` (an IP address). Should you?

choices.label: Yes; modern receivers accept IP-as-MX even though the spec discourages it.
choices.rationale: Many receivers reject IP-as-MX outright. The mail bounces silently.

choices: No. MX targets must be hostnames, not IPs. Ask the vendor for a hostname.

choices.label: Yes; wrap the IP in square brackets for the IP-literal form.
choices.rationale: Square-bracket IP literals work in SMTP EHLO, not in MX record targets.

choices.label: Only if the IP is in the same /24 as the receiver's network.
choices.rationale: Network proximity has nothing to do with MX target format rules.

questions.explanation: RFC 5321 requires MX targets to be hostnames with A/AAAA records, not bare IPs. Some DNS hosts let you save the IP form, but many receivers reject it and mail bounces. Ask the vendor for a hostname; reputable mail providers publish one.

questions.prompt: MX records can safely point at a CNAME, as long as the CNAME resolves to a host with A/AAAA records.
questions.explanation: RFC 2181 prohibits MX-pointing-at-CNAME. Some receivers follow the chain; others reject. The defensive practice is to point MX directly at the canonical FQDN that has A/AAAA records, not via a CNAME indirection.
